foundation_variants.name: In-Place Idiomatic Rewrite
foundation_variants.description: Preserves module structure and public API but rewrites bodies idiomatically: function heads replace case chains, |> replaces temp vars, with replaces nested conditionals, pattern destructure replaces Map.get.
foundation_variants.skill_md:
---
name: refactor-philosophy--in-place-idiomatic
description: Refactors non-idiomatic Elixir (Ruby/Java-style if/case chains, defensive nil checks, manual loops) into idiomatic Elixir using function heads, guards, pipes, with, and pattern-matched destructures. Use when asked to improve Elixir code style or to refactor imperative patterns. NOT for writing new code from scratch.
---

# In-Place Idiomatic Rewrite

## Quick Start
Keep the module structure and API. Rewrite the bodies.

## When to use
- Refactoring imperative Elixir into idiomatic Elixir
- Fixing Ruby/Java-style code written by LLMs
- Reviewing PRs for idiomatic style

## Workflow
1. Identify if/case/cond chains → rewrite as function heads with guards
2. Identify intermediate temp vars → rewrite as pipes
3. Identify nested conditionals → rewrite as `with <- ... do ... else ...`
4. Identify `is_nil` / `x && x.field` → rewrite as function head pattern match
5. Identify Map.get chains → rewrite as map destructure in head

## Common mistakes
- Leaving one `case` where a function head would work
- Keeping `is_nil` as a "defensive" fallback
- Using `Enum.reduce` where `for` comprehension is cleaner

capability_variants.name: Multi-Clause Function Heads
capability_variants.description: Replaces case statements with multiple function heads matching different patterns. One head per pattern, not a single head with case inside.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: function-head-pattern-matching--multi-clause
description: Replaces case chains with multi-clause function heads.
---

# Multi-Clause Function Heads

## Quick Start
`def foo(%{type: :a}), do: ...; def foo(%{type: :b}), do: ...` replaces `def foo(x), do: case x.type do ...`

## Workflow
1. Each branch of the case becomes a separate function head
2. Put the pattern in the head, not in a case inside the body
3. Order matters — Elixir tries heads top-to-bottom

capability_variants.name: When Guards
capability_variants.description: Uses `when` guards in function heads to narrow patterns beyond what the head itself can match. Composes with and/or/not.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: guard-clauses--when
description: when guards in function heads.
---

# When Guards

## Quick Start
`def foo(n) when is_integer(n) and n > 0, do: ...` narrows the match beyond the head.

## Workflow
1. Use guards for type/shape/range checks
2. Compose with `and`, `or`, `not`
3. Only use allowed guard functions (is_*, ==, >, <, etc.)

capability_variants.name: No Temp Vars In Pipes
capability_variants.description: Chains transformations with |> instead of intermediate variables. Breaks the pipe only when a side effect or explicit name is needed.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: pipe-operator-flows--no-temps
description: |> pipe operator chains over intermediate temp vars.
---

# No Temp Vars In Pipes

## Quick Start
`input |> step1() |> step2() |> step3()` replaces `a = step1(input); b = step2(a); step3(b)`.

## When to break the pipe
- Side effects (log, persist) that don't return the flow value
- When an explicit intermediate name improves readability

capability_variants.name: Happy-Path With Expressions
capability_variants.description: Uses `with` for multi-step flows where each step can fail. `else` clause handles all error branches in one place.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: with-expressions--happy-path
description: with expressions for multi-step error handling.
---

# Happy-Path With Expressions

## Quick Start
```elixir
with {:ok, user} <- fetch_user(id),
     {:ok, post} <- create_post(user, params),
     :ok <- notify_subscribers(post) do
  {:ok, post}
else
  {:error, _} = error -> error
end
```

## When to use
- 3+ steps that each return `{:ok, _}` / `{:error, _}`
- Nested case chains you want to flatten

capability_variants.name: Tail-Recursive With Pattern Matching
capability_variants.description: Base case and step case are separate function heads. Step case uses pattern-matched destructure. Tail-recursive.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: recursive-functions--tail-recursive
description: Tail-recursive functions with pattern-matched base/step cases.
---

# Tail-Recursive With Pattern Matching

## Workflow
1. Base case: `def foo([], acc), do: acc`
2. Step case: `def foo([h | t], acc), do: foo(t, [h * 2 | acc])`
3. Tail position means the recursive call is the last expression

capability_variants.name: Enum First, Recursion When Needed
capability_variants.description: Prefers Enum.* functions when they fit. Falls back to explicit recursion only when Enum cannot express the logic (e.g., early termination, paired iteration, accumulator shape).
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: enum-vs-recursion-choice--enum-first
description: Choose Enum over explicit recursion unless Enum cannot express the logic.
---

# Enum First, Recursion When Needed

## Quick Start
Enum is faster, clearer, and more familiar. Use recursion only when necessary.

## When to use recursion instead of Enum
- Early termination (`Enum.reduce_while` is an alternative)
- Paired iteration over two lists
- Non-list data structures
- When the accumulator shape is non-trivial

capability_variants.name: Destructure In Function Head
capability_variants.description: Extracts fields directly in the function head using `%{field: val}` or `%Struct{field: val}`. Avoids Map.get/Map.fetch noise.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: map-and-struct-destructuring--in-head
description: Destructure map/struct fields in function heads.
---

# Destructure In Function Head

## Quick Start
`def process(%User{id: id, email: email} = user)` instead of `def process(user), do: (id = user.id; email = user.email; ...)`

## Workflow
1. Put the pattern in the head
2. Bind the whole value with `= user` if you need it
3. Use struct destructure for schema-backed types

capability_variants.name: Binary Prefix Pattern
capability_variants.description: Uses `<<"prefix", rest::binary>>` to match string prefixes. Replaces String.starts_with? + slice patterns for parsing.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: binary-pattern-matching-basic--prefix-match
description: <<"prefix", rest::binary>> binary patterns for string parsing.
---

# Binary Prefix Pattern

## Quick Start
`def parse(<<"http://", rest::binary>>), do: ...` replaces `def parse(url), do: if String.starts_with?(url, "http://"), do: ...`

capability_variants.name: Heads With Guards Replace Cond
capability_variants.description: Rewrites cond chains as multi-clause function heads with guards. Rewrites nested if chains as with expressions.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: cond-and-if-reduction--heads-with-guards
description: Replace cond chains with function heads + guards.
---

# Heads With Guards Replace Cond

## Workflow
1. Each `cond` branch becomes a function head with a guard
2. Nested `if` chains become `with` expressions
3. Single `if-else` with different types is usually a sign you need pattern matching instead

capability_variants.name: Pattern Match Over Nil Check
capability_variants.description: Replaces `is_nil(x)` and `x && x.field` patterns with function heads that match on the presence or absence of the value.
capability_variants.skill_md:
---
name: defensive-nil-checks-elimination--pattern-match
description: Eliminates defensive nil checks via pattern matching.
---

# Pattern Match Over Nil Check

## Quick Start
`def display_name(%User{name: name}) when is_binary(name) and name != "", do: name; def display_name(_), do: "Anonymous"` replaces `def display_name(user), do: if user && user.name && user.name != "", do: user.name, else: "Anonymous"`.

## Common mistakes
- Leaving `is_nil` as a "safety net"
- Pattern-matching only the nil case and delegating to an if
